resources.help.title: Help
resources.help.subtitle: Overview of how you can work in the application to build up and improve your budget over time.
resources.help.introductionBody1: Budget Advisor is designed to work both as a quick planning tool and as a more detailed budgeting tool when you want to build up history over time.
resources.help.introductionBody2: You can start from today with no history at all, or gradually build up around twelve months of data using imports, recurring expenses, loans, savings, and manual adjustments.
resources.help.introductionBody3: The goal is that you should get value from the application early and then improve the quality of the budget as more data becomes available.
resources.help.startingPointsSubtitle: There is no single correct workflow. Choose the one that fits your data and how quickly you want to get started.
resources.help.startPoint1Body: Register only what you know right now, such as members, income, fixed costs, loans, credits, and savings. That is often enough to get a first useful budget overview.
resources.help.startPoint2Body: Import transactions from your bank and work backward month by month. This gives better input for averages, budgeting, and analysis.
resources.help.startPoint3Body: If you do not use bank data, you can still create a useful budget by entering reasonable estimates for income, expenses, recurring costs, and loans.
resources.help.startPoint4Body: Recurring expenses and loan functions can be used both backward and forward in time to create a more complete budget model.
resources.help.workflowSubtitle: A practical way to build up the budget step by step.
resources.help.workflowStep1: Add household members and connect income to the correct member.
resources.help.workflowStep2: Import transactions or register a few months manually.
resources.help.workflowStep3: Categorize income and expenses so the data becomes useful for budgeting and analysis.
resources.help.workflowStep4: Create recurring expenses where appropriate, such as rent, broadband, insurance, or subscriptions.
resources.help.workflowStep5: Register housing loans, transport loans, leasing, credits, and savings.
resources.help.workflowStep6: Use Dashboard and Analysis to find patterns, transfers, Swish entries, and anomalies.
resources.help.workflowStep7: Adjust categories, member assignments, and assumptions until the budget feels reasonable.
resources.help.sampleDataIntro: Two backup files are available for testing Budget Advisor with the fictional household Alice & Bob. All related files are located in the `sample-data` folder in the repository.
resources.help.sampleDataOption1Body2: Then import all `*.csv` files from the `sample-data` folder.
resources.help.sampleDataOption1Body3: This will create three months of transaction history for both Alice and Bob.
resources.help.sampleDataOption2Title: Option 2: Skip import and go directly to analysis/testing
resources.help.sampleDataOption2Body2: The data in this backup intentionally contains a few things that need to be adjusted.
resources.help.sampleDataOption2Body3: This is by design, so you can try the Analysis features in the application.
resources.help.sampleDataOption2Body4: In particular, you can test removing transfer posts.
resources.help.sampleDataOption2Body5: You can also inspect the Swish posts and assign names to them if desired.
resources.help.sampleDataOption2Body6: There are three mobile numbers in the sample data: one for Alice, one for Bob, and one for their child.
resources.help.tabsSubtitle: Each main tab has a clear role in the workflow.
resources.help.tab.start: Dashboard gives you a quick overview of budget, key metrics, debts, and savings. It is a good place to check whether the overall picture looks reasonable.
resources.help.tab.analysis: Analysis is used to find and clean data. It includes Transfers, Swish, and Query to identify patterns and remove unwanted entries.
resources.help.tab.household: Income is used for household members and income registration. Here you can connect income to members and understand how household income is distributed.
resources.help.tab.housing: Housing collects housing costs, recurring housing expenses, and housing loans. You can also build up interest and amortization over time using binding periods.
resources.help.tab.transport: Transport is used for vehicles, leasing, transport loans, and transport-related costs. This makes it easier to understand the full transport economy.
resources.help.tab.expenses: Expenses is the general area for costs that do not belong under housing or transport. Here you can register regular expenses and recurring entries.
resources.help.tab.credits: Credits is used to track debts connected to credits and other running debt balances that affect your overall financial picture.
resources.help.tab.savings: Savings is used to track savings over time and affects several calculations in Dashboard and the budget overview.
resources.help.tab.import: Import is used to load bank transactions, preview rows, assign a member for the import, and later correct member assignment through the import log.
resources.help.tab.settings: Settings contains language, currency, backup, local storage, log, and other functions for managing the application's data.
resources.help.recurringBody1: Recurring expenses are important when you want to build a realistic budget without registering every single row manually. They are useful for rent, electricity, broadband, insurance, and subscriptions.
resources.help.recurringBody2: The loan functions are intended to model both current state and development over time, such as remaining debt, current amortization, binding periods, and historical or future loan costs.
resources.help.philosophyBody1: The application is intended as a planning tool rather than strict bookkeeping. Exact imported data works well, but estimates also work well.
resources.help.philosophyBody2: You can mix real data and assumptions, start simple, and then refine the model gradually. The important thing is that the budget becomes useful for decisions and planning.
